title: Queen Charlotte

* [George IV](/wiki/George_IV "George IV") * [Prince Frederick, Duke of York and Albany](/wiki/Prince_Frederick,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany "Prince Frederick, Duke of York and Albany") * [William IV](/wiki/William_IV "William IV") * [Charlotte, Queen of Württemberg](/wiki/Charlotte,_Queen_of_W%C3%BCrttemberg "Charlotte, Queen of Württemberg") * [Prince Edward, Duke of Kent and Strathearn](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Kent_and_Strathearn "Prince Edward, Duke of Kent and Strathearn") * [Princess Augusta Sophia](/wiki/Princess_Augusta_Sophia "Princess Augusta Sophia") * [Elizabeth, Landgravine of Hesse-Homburg](/wiki/Elizabeth,_Landgravine_of_Hesse-Homburg "Elizabeth, Landgravine of Hesse-Homburg") * [Ernest Augustus, King of Hanover](/wiki/Ernest_Augustus,_King_of_Hanover "Ernest Augustus, King of Hanover") * [Prince Augustus Frederick, Duke of Sussex](/wiki/Prince_Augustus_Frederick,_Duke_of_Sussex "Prince Augustus Frederick, Duke of Sussex") * [Prince Adolphus, Duke of Cambridge](/wiki/Prince_Adolphus,_Duke_of_Cambridge "Prince Adolphus, Duke of Cambridge") * [Princess Mary, Duchess of Gloucester and Edinburgh](/wiki/Princess_Mary,_Duchess_of_Gloucester_and_Edinburgh "Princess Mary, Duchess of Gloucester and Edinburgh") * [Princess Sophia](/wiki/Princess_Sophia_of_the_United_Kingdom "Princess Sophia of the United Kingdom") * [Prince Octavius](/wiki/Prince_Octavius "Prince Octavius") * [Prince Alfred](/wiki/Prince_Alfred_of_Great_Britain "Prince Alfred of Great Britain") * [Princess Amelia](/wiki/Princess_Amelia_of_the_United_Kingdom "Princess Amelia of the United Kingdom")
[Duke Charles Louis Frederick of Mecklenburg, Prince of Mirow](/wiki/Duke_Charles_Louis_Frederick_of_Mecklenburg,_Prince_of_Mirow "Duke Charles Louis Frederick of Mecklenburg, Prince of Mirow")
[Princess Elisabeth Albertine of Saxe-Hildburghausen](/wiki/Princess_Elisabeth_Albertine_of_Saxe-Hildburghausen "Princess Elisabeth Albertine of Saxe-Hildburghausen")

(Sophia Charlotte; 19 May 1744 – 17 November 1818) was [Queen of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/List_of_British_royal_consorts "List of British royal consorts") as the wife of [King George III](/wiki/King_George_III "King George III") from their marriage on 8 September 1761 until her death in 1818. The [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") unified [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") and [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") into the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"). As George's wife, she was also [Electress](/wiki/Electress "Electress") of [Hanover](/wiki/Electorate_of_Hanover "Electorate of Hanover") until becoming [Queen of Hanover](/wiki/Queen_of_Hanover "Queen of Hanover") on 12 October 1814. Charlotte was Britain's longest-serving [queen consort](/wiki/Queen_consort "Queen consort"), serving for 57 years and 70 days.

**Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz** (Sophia Charlotte; 19 May 1744 – 17 November 1818) was [Queen of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/List_of_British_royal_consorts "List of British royal consorts") as the wife of [King George III](/wiki/King_George_III "King George III") from their marriage on 8 September 1761 until her death in 1818. The [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") unified [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") and [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") into the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"). As George's wife, she was also [Electress](/wiki/Electress "Electress") of [Hanover](/wiki/Electorate_of_Hanover "Electorate of Hanover") until becoming [Queen of Hanover](/wiki/Queen_of_Hanover "Queen of Hanover") on 12 October 1814. Charlotte was Britain's longest-serving [queen consort](/wiki/Queen_consort "Queen consort"), serving for 57 years and 70 days.

Charlotte was born into the ruling family of [Mecklenburg-Strelitz](/wiki/Mecklenburg-Strelitz "Mecklenburg-Strelitz"), a [duchy](/wiki/Duchy "Duchy") in northern Germany. In 1760, the young and unmarried George III inherited the British throne. As Charlotte was a minor German princess with no interest in politics, the King considered her a suitable consort, and they married in 1761. The marriage lasted 57 years, during which Charlotte bore 15 children, 13 of whom survived to adulthood. They included two future British monarchs, [George IV](/wiki/George_IV "George IV") and [William IV](/wiki/William_IV "William IV"); as well as [Charlotte, Princess Royal](/wiki/Charlotte,_Princess_Royal "Charlotte, Princess Royal"), who became [Queen of Württemberg](/wiki/Queen_of_W%C3%BCrttemberg "Queen of Württemberg"); and [Prince Ernest Augustus](/wiki/Ernest_Augustus,_King_of_Hanover "Ernest Augustus, King of Hanover"), who became [King of Hanover](/wiki/King_of_Hanover "King of Hanover").

Charlotte was a patron of the arts and an amateur [botanist](/wiki/Botanist "Botanist") who helped expand [Kew Gardens](/wiki/Kew_Gardens "Kew Gardens"). She introduced the [Christmas tree](/wiki/Christmas_tree "Christmas tree") to Britain, decorating one for a Christmas party for children of [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor,_Berkshire "Windsor, Berkshire") in 1800. She was distressed by her husband's bouts of [physical and mental illness](/wiki/Illness_of_George_III "Illness of George III"), which became permanent in later life. Charlotte was deeply shocked by the events of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") and of the ensuing [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), which threatened the safety and sovereignty of her homeland. Her eldest son, George, was appointed [prince regent](/wiki/Prince_regent "Prince regent") in 1811 due to the increasing severity of the King's illness. Charlotte died at [Kew Palace](/wiki/Kew_Palace "Kew Palace") in November 1818, with several of her children at her side. George III died a little over a year later, probably unaware of his wife's death.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charlotte_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Mirow_Schloss.jpg/250px-Mirow_Schloss.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mirow_Schloss.jpg)

Charlotte's birthplace in [Mirow](/wiki/Mirow "Mirow")

Charlotte was born on 19 May 1744. She was the youngest daughter of [Duke Charles Louis Frederick of Mecklenburg, Prince of Mirow](/wiki/Duke_Charles_Louis_Frederick_of_Mecklenburg,_Prince_of_Mirow "Duke Charles Louis Frederick of Mecklenburg, Prince of Mirow") (1708–1752), and his wife [Princess Elisabeth Albertine of Saxe-Hildburghausen](/wiki/Princess_Elisabeth_Albertine_of_Saxe-Hildburghausen "Princess Elisabeth Albertine of Saxe-Hildburghausen") (1713–1761). [Mecklenburg-Strelitz](/wiki/Mecklenburg-Strelitz "Mecklenburg-Strelitz") was a small north-German duchy in the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire").[[2]](#cite_note-4)

The children of Duke Charles were all born at the Unteres Schloss (Lower Castle) in [Mirow](/wiki/Mirow "Mirow").[[3]](#cite_note-5)
According to diplomatic reports at the time of her engagement to [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") in 1761, Charlotte had received "a very mediocre education"[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser200516-6) and contemporary Britons including [Elizabeth Montagu](/wiki/Elizabeth_Montagu "Elizabeth Montagu") expressed anxiety about the supposed provinciality of Charlotte's upbringing.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-7): 20 Her parents hired notable individuals to tutor their children, among them [Gottlob Burchard Genzmer](/wiki/Gottlob_Burchard_Genzmer "Gottlob Burchard Genzmer") and [Friderike Elisabeth von Grabow](/wiki/Friderike_Elisabeth_von_Grabow "Friderike Elisabeth von Grabow").[[5]](#cite_note-:1-7): 29 Charlotte received instruction in literature, [botany](/wiki/Botany "Botany"), [natural history](/wiki/Natural_history "Natural history"), and [languages](/wiki/Languages "Languages") including French, Italian, and Latin. She was also taught traditional pursuits for upper-class girls, including embroidery, dancing, singing, [household management](/wiki/Household_management "Household management") and [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion") – the latter taught by a [priest](/wiki/Priest "Priest"). Charlotte was also taught to play the [harpsichord](/wiki/Harpsichord "Harpsichord") by composer Johann Georg Linike.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-7): 30 The family lived a modest life at Mirow; only after her brother [Adolphus Frederick](/wiki/Adolphus_Frederick_IV,_Duke_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz "Adolphus Frederick IV, Duke of Mecklenburg-Strelitz") succeeded to the ducal throne, in 1752, did Charlotte gain any experience of princely duties and of court life.[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8)

## Marriage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charlotte_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Marriage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Johann_Georg_Ziesenis_-_Queen_Charlotte_when_Princess%2C_Royal_Collection.jpg/250px-Johann_Georg_Ziesenis_-_Queen_Charlotte_when_Princess%2C_Royal_Collection.jpg)](/wiki/File:Johann_Georg_Ziesenis_-_Queen_Charlotte_when_Princess,_Royal_Collection.jpg)

Charlotte with a servant by [Johann Georg Ziesenis](/wiki/Johann_Georg_Ziesenis "Johann Georg Ziesenis"), c. 1761

When George III succeeded to the throne of [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") upon the 1760 death of his grandfather, [George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain"), he was 22 years old and unmarried. His mother, [Princess Augusta of Saxe-Gotha](/wiki/Princess_Augusta_of_Saxe-Gotha "Princess Augusta of Saxe-Gotha"), and his advisors were eager to have him settled in marriage.

Charlotte was not originally considered as a potential bride, but the Hanoverian Minister in London, Baron Philip Adolphus von Münchausen, suggested her as a candidate, likely due to the positive relations between Hanover and Mecklenburg-Strelitz.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-7): 36 The 17-year-old Charlotte appealed as a prospective consort partly because she had been brought up in an insignificant north German duchy and, therefore, would probably have had no experience or interest in power politics or party intrigues. That proved to be the case; to make sure, George III instructed her shortly after their wedding "not to meddle", a precept she dutifully followed.[[7]](#cite_note-9)

The King announced to his Council in July 1761, according to the usual form, his intention to wed the Princess, after which a party of escorts, led by [the Earl Harcourt](/wiki/Simon_Harcourt,_1st_Earl_Harcourt "Simon Harcourt, 1st Earl Harcourt"), departed for Germany to bring Princess Charlotte to England. They reached [Strelitz](/wiki/Duchy_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz "Duchy of Mecklenburg-Strelitz") on 14 August 1761, and were received the next day by Duke Adolphus Frederick IV, Charlotte's brother, at which time the marriage contract was signed by him on the one hand and Lord Harcourt on the other.[[8]](#cite_note-10)[[9]](#cite_note-11) Charlotte's mother had died on 29 June, after giving encouragement to the betrothal following a correspondence with George III's mother, Princess Augusta.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-7): 45

Three days of public celebrations followed, and on 17 August 1761, Charlotte set out for Britain, accompanied by Adolphus Frederick and the British escort party, among them one of Charlotte's new Ladies of the Bedchamber, [Elizabeth Hamilton, 1st Baroness Hamilton of Hameldon](/wiki/Elizabeth_Hamilton,_1st_Baroness_Hamilton_of_Hameldon "Elizabeth Hamilton, 1st Baroness Hamilton of Hameldon"). On 22 August, they reached [Cuxhaven](/wiki/Cuxhaven "Cuxhaven"), where a small fleet awaited to convey them to England. The voyage was extremely difficult; the party encountered three storms at sea and landed at [Harwich](/wiki/Harwich "Harwich") only on 7 September. They set out at once for London, spent that night in [Witham](/wiki/Witham "Witham"), at the residence of [Lord Abercorn](/wiki/James_Hamilton,_8th_Earl_of_Abercorn "James Hamilton, 8th Earl of Abercorn"), and arrived at 3:30 pm the next day at [St. James's Palace](/wiki/St._James%27s_Palace "St. James's Palace") in London. They were received by the King and his family at the garden gate, which marked the first meeting of the bride and groom.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-7): 64

At 9:00 pm that same evening (8 September 1761), within six hours of her arrival, Charlotte was married to George III. The ceremony was performed at the [Chapel Royal](/wiki/Chapel_Royal "Chapel Royal"), St. James's Palace, by the [archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury"), [Thomas Secker](/wiki/Thomas_Secker "Thomas Secker").[[10]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald_pp._32-33-12) Only the royal family, the party who had travelled from Germany, and a handful of guests were present.[[10]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald_pp._32-33-12) [George III and Charlotte's coronation](/wiki/Coronation_of_George_III_and_Charlotte "Coronation of George III and Charlotte") was held at [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey") a fortnight later on 22 September, after a brief honeymoon at [Richmond Lodge](/wiki/Richmond_Lodge "Richmond Lodge").[[5]](#cite_note-:1-7): 69

## Queen consort

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charlotte_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Queen consort")]

Upon her wedding day, Charlotte spoke little English. However, she quickly learned the language, albeit speaking with a strong German accent. One observer commented, "She is timid at first but talks a lot, when she is among people she knows."[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser200517-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Johan_Zoffany_-_Queen_Charlotte_%281744-1818%29_with_her_Two_Eldest_Sons_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Johan_Zoffany_-_Queen_Charlotte_%281744-1818%29_with_her_Two_Eldest_Sons_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Johan_Zoffany_-_Queen_Charlotte_(1744-1818)_with_her_Two_Eldest_Sons_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*Queen Charlotte with her Two Eldest Sons*, [Johan Zoffany](/wiki/Johan_Zoffany "Johan Zoffany"), 1765

Less than a year after the marriage, on 12 August 1762, the Queen gave birth to her first child, [George, Prince of Wales](/wiki/George_IV "George IV"). In the course of their marriage, the couple became the parents of 15 children,[[12]](#cite_note-14) all but two of whom ([Octavius](/wiki/Prince_Octavius_of_Great_Britain "Prince Octavius of Great Britain") and [Alfred](/wiki/Prince_Alfred_of_Great_Britain "Prince Alfred of Great Britain")) survived into adulthood.[[13]](#cite_note-15)[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeir2008300-16)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHolt1820251-17)

[St James's Palace](/wiki/St_James%27s_Palace "St James's Palace") functioned as the official residence of the royal couple, but the King had recently purchased a nearby property, [Buckingham House](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace"), located at the western end of [St James's Park](/wiki/St_James%27s_Park "St James's Park"). More private and compact, the new property stood amid rolling parkland not far from St James's Palace. Around 1762, the King and Queen moved to this residence, which was originally intended as a private retreat. The Queen came to favour this residence, spending so much of her time there that it came to be known as The Queen's House. Indeed, in 1775, an Act of Parliament settled the property on Charlotte in exchange for her rights to [Somerset House](/wiki/Somerset_House "Somerset House").[[16]](#cite_note-18) Most of the couple's 15 children were born in Buckingham House, although St James's Palace remained the official and ceremonial royal residence.[[17]](#cite_note-19)[[c]](#cite_note-20)[[d]](#cite_note-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Charlotte1767Cotes.jpg/250px-Charlotte1767Cotes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charlotte1767Cotes.jpg)

In 1767, [Francis Cotes](/wiki/Francis_Cotes "Francis Cotes") drew a pastel of Queen Charlotte with her eldest daughter, [Charlotte, Princess Royal](/wiki/Charlotte,_Princess_Royal "Charlotte, Princess Royal"). [Lady Mary Coke](/wiki/Lady_Mary_Coke "Lady Mary Coke") called the likeness "so like that it could not be mistaken for any other person".[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevey19778–9-22)

During her first years in Great Britain, Charlotte's strained relationship with her mother-in-law, Augusta, caused her difficulty in adapting to the life of the British court.[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8) Augusta interfered with Charlotte's efforts to establish social contacts by insisting on rigid court etiquette.[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8) Furthermore, Augusta appointed many of Charlotte's staff, among whom several were expected to report to Augusta about Charlotte's behaviour.[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8) Charlotte turned to her German companions for friends, notably her close confidante [Juliane von Schwellenberg](/wiki/Juliane_von_Schwellenberg "Juliane von Schwellenberg").[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8) Charlotte's personal correspondence with her brother [Charles II, Grand Duke of Mecklenburg-Strelitz](/wiki/Charles_II,_Grand_Duke_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz "Charles II, Grand Duke of Mecklenburg-Strelitz"), revealed the depth of her loneliness and of her frustration with the regulations of royal life.[[19]](#cite_note-23)

The King enjoyed country pursuits and riding and preferred to keep his family's residence as much as possible in the then-rural towns of [Kew](/wiki/Kew "Kew") and [Richmond](/wiki/Richmond,_London "Richmond, London"). He favoured an informal and relaxed domestic life, to the dismay of some courtiers more accustomed to displays of grandeur and strict protocol. [Lady Mary Coke](/wiki/Lady_Mary_Coke "Lady Mary Coke") was indignant on hearing, in July 1769, that the King, the Queen, her visiting brother [Prince Ernest](/wiki/Duke_Ernest_Gottlob_of_Mecklenburg "Duke Ernest Gottlob of Mecklenburg") and [Lady Effingham](/wiki/Elizabeth_Howard,_Countess_of_Effingham "Elizabeth Howard, Countess of Effingham") had gone for a walk through Richmond by themselves without any servants: "I am not satisfied in my mind about the propriety of a Queen walking in town unattended."[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser200523-24)

From 1778, the royal family spent much of their time at a newly constructed residence, the Queen's Lodge at Windsor, opposite [Windsor Castle](/wiki/Windsor_Castle "Windsor Castle"), in [Windsor Great Park](/wiki/Windsor_Great_Park "Windsor Great Park"), where the King enjoyed hunting deer.[[21]](#cite_note-25) The Queen was responsible for the interior decoration of their new residence, described by a friend of the royal family and diarist [Mary Delany](/wiki/Mary_Delany "Mary Delany"): "The entrance into the first room was dazzling, all furnished with beautiful Indian paper, chairs covered with different embroideries of the liveliest colours, glasses, tables, sconces, in the best taste, the whole calculated to give the greatest cheerfulness to the place."[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser200523-24)

Charlotte treated her children's attendants with friendly warmth, which is reflected in this note she wrote to her daughters' assistant governess, Mary Hamilton:

> My dear Miss Hamilton, What can I have to say? Not much indeed! But to wish you a good morning, in the pretty blue and white room where I had the pleasure to sit and read with you *The Hermit*, a poem which is such a favourite with me that I have read it twice this summer. Oh! What a blessing to keep good company! Very likely I should not have been acquainted with either poet or poem was it not for you.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser200572-26)

Charlotte did have some influence on political affairs through the King. Her influence was discreet and indirect, as demonstrated in the correspondence with her brother Charles. She used her closeness with George III to keep herself informed and to make recommendations for offices.[[23]](#cite_note-Clarissa_Campbell_Orr_2004-27) Apparently her recommendations were not direct; she on one occasion, in 1779, asked her brother Charles to burn her letter because the King suspected that a person she had recently recommended for a post was the client of a woman who sold offices.[[23]](#cite_note-Clarissa_Campbell_Orr_2004-27) Charlotte particularly interested herself in German issues. She took an interest in the [War of the Bavarian Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Bavarian_Succession "War of the Bavarian Succession") (1778–1779), and it is possible that it was due to her efforts that the King supported British intervention in the continuing conflict between [Joseph II](/wiki/Joseph_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Joseph II, Holy Roman Emperor") and [Charles Theodore of Bavaria](/wiki/Charles_Theodore,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Charles Theodore, Elector of Bavaria") in 1785.[[23]](#cite_note-Clarissa_Campbell_Orr_2004-27)

## Husband's first period of illness

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charlotte_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Husband's first period of illness")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Dance_-_Queen_Charlotte%2C_Uppark_House.jpg/250px-Dance_-_Queen_Charlotte%2C_Uppark_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dance_-_Queen_Charlotte,_Uppark_House.jpg)

*[Portrait of Queen Charlotte](/wiki/Portrait_of_Queen_Charlotte_(Dance-Holland) "Portrait of Queen Charlotte (Dance-Holland)")* by [Nathaniel Dance-Holland](/wiki/Nathaniel_Dance-Holland "Nathaniel Dance-Holland"), c. 1768

Some contemporaries, including [Charlotte Papendiek](/wiki/Charlotte_Papendiek "Charlotte Papendiek"), one of Charlotte's attendants, believed that George III first suffered from [mental illness](/wiki/Illness_of_George_III "Illness of George III") in 1765.[[24]](#cite_note-28) However, the royal governess, [Lady Charlotte Finch](/wiki/Lady_Charlotte_Finch "Lady Charlotte Finch") recorded that the king was merely ill with a fever; unlike Mrs Papendiek, who was in July 1765, Lady Charlotte was present in the royal household at the time.[[25]](#cite_note-29) Mrs Papendiek claimed in her memoirs that Princess Augusta tried to keep Charlotte unaware of the situation in order to establish herself as regent.[[26]](#cite_note-30) The Regency Bill of 1765 stated that if the King should become permanently unable to rule, Charlotte was to act as regent until the Prince of Wales came of age.

George III's bout of physical and mental illness started in October 1788 and lasted until March 1789. Charlotte was deeply distressed by the change in her husband's behaviour. The writer [Frances Burney](/wiki/Frances_Burney "Frances Burney"), at that time one of the Queen's attendants, overheard her moaning to herself with "desponding sound": "What will become of me? What will become of me?"[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser2005116-31) When the King collapsed one night, she refused to be left alone with him and successfully insisted that she be given her own bedroom. When the doctor, [Richard Warren](/wiki/Richard_Warren_(physician) "Richard Warren (physician)"), was called, she was not informed and was not given the opportunity to speak with him about it. When told by the Prince of Wales that the King was to be removed to Kew, but that she should move to Queen's House or to Windsor, she successfully insisted that she accompany her spouse to Kew, telling her son "Where the king is, *there I shall be*."[[28]](#cite_note-32) However, she and her daughters were taken to Kew separately from the King and lived secluded from him during his illness. They regularly visited him, but the visits tended to be uncomfortable, as he had a tendency to embrace them and refuse to let them go.[[29]](#cite_note-33)

During the 1788 illness of the King, a conflict arose between the Queen and the Prince of Wales, who suspected one another of desiring to assume the regency should the illness of the King become permanent, resulting in him being declared unfit to rule. Charlotte suspected her son of a plan to have the King declared insane with the assistance of Doctor Warren, and to take over the regency.[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8) Prince George's followers, notably Sir Gilbert Ellis, in turn suspected the Queen of a plan to have the King declared sane with the assistance of Doctor [Francis Willis](/wiki/Francis_Willis_(physician) "Francis Willis (physician)") and Prime Minister [William Pitt](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Younger "William Pitt the Younger"), so that he could have her appointed regent should he fall ill again, and then have him declared insane again and assume the regency.[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8) According to Doctor Warren, Doctor Willis had pressed him to declare the King sane on the orders of the Queen.[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/King_George_and_Earl_Howe_aboard_Qn_Charlotte.jpg/250px-King_George_and_Earl_Howe_aboard_Qn_Charlotte.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_George_and_Earl_Howe_aboard_Qn_Charlotte.jpg)

*[Visit of George III to Howe's Flagship the Queen Charlotte](/wiki/Visit_of_George_III_to_Howe%27s_Flagship_the_Queen_Charlotte "Visit of George III to Howe's Flagship the Queen Charlotte")* by [Henry Perronet Briggs](/wiki/Henry_Perronet_Briggs "Henry Perronet Briggs"), 1828. George and Charlotte with [Admiral Howe](/wiki/Richard_Howe,_1st_Earl_Howe "Richard Howe, 1st Earl Howe") on [HMS *Queen Charlotte*](/wiki/HMS_Queen_Charlotte_(1790) "HMS Queen Charlotte (1790)"), 1794

In the Regency Bill of 1789, the Prince of Wales was declared regent should the King become permanently insane, but it also placed the King himself, his court and minor children under the Queen's guardianship.[[30]](#cite_note-34) The conflict around the regency led to serious discord between the Prince of Wales and his mother.[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8) In an argument he accused her of having sided with his enemies, while she called him the enemy of the King.[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8) Their conflict became public when she refused to invite him to the concert held in celebration of the recovery of the King, which created a scandal.[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8) During this period, Charlotte was caricatured in satirical prints that depicted her as an unnatural mother and a creature of the Prime Minister.[[31]](#cite_note-35) In January 1789 *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* accused the Opposition of beginning "a most scurrilous attack on the queen, not only by private conversation, but through the medium of the prints in their interest".[[32]](#cite_note-36) Charlotte and the Prince of Wales finally reconciled, on her initiative, in March 1791.[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8)

As a result of the king's illness in 1788–89 and of the public attacks on her character, the Queen's personality altered: she developed a terrible temper and no longer enjoyed appearing in public, not even at the musical concerts she had so loved; and her relationships with her adult children became strained.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser2005pp._112–379_passim-37) From 1792 she found some relief from her worry about her husband by planning the gardens and decoration of a new residence for herself, [Frogmore House](/wiki/Frogmore_House "Frogmore House"), in Windsor Home Park.[[34]](#cite_note-38)

When the king's mental health declined again in 1804, it caused a serious rupture in the royal marriage. Despite the entreaties of her daughters and of the king's physicians, Charlotte slept in a separate bedroom, had her meals separate from the king, and avoided spending time alone with him.[[35]](#cite_note-39)

## Interests and patronage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charlotte_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Interests and patronage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Margaret_botany.jpg/250px-Margaret_botany.jpg)](/wiki/File:Margaret_botany.jpg)

"Patroness of Botany, and of the Fine Arts"

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Queen-charlotte-1744-1818.jpg/250px-Queen-charlotte-1744-1818.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen-charlotte-1744-1818.jpg)

Queen Charlotte in Robes of State, by [Joshua Reynolds](/wiki/Joshua_Reynolds "Joshua Reynolds"), 1779

Charlotte and her husband were music connoisseurs, who gave special honour to German artists and composers. They were admirers of the music of [George Frideric Handel](/wiki/George_Frideric_Handel "George Frideric Handel").[[36]](#cite_note-40)

In April 1764, [Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart](/wiki/Wolfgang_Amadeus_Mozart "Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart"), then aged eight, arrived in Britain with his family as part of their [grand tour of Europe](/wiki/Mozart_family_grand_tour "Mozart family grand tour") and remained until July 1765.[[37]](#cite_note-41) The Mozarts were summoned to court on 19 May and played before a limited circle from six to ten o'clock. [Johann Christian Bach](/wiki/Johann_Christian_Bach "Johann Christian Bach"), eleventh son of the great [Johann Sebastian Bach](/wiki/Johann_Sebastian_Bach "Johann Sebastian Bach"), was then music-master to the Queen. He put difficult works of Handel, J. S. Bach, and [Carl Friedrich Abel](/wiki/Carl_Friedrich_Abel "Carl Friedrich Abel") before the boy: he played them all [at sight](/wiki/Sight_reading "Sight reading"), to the amazement of those present.[[38]](#cite_note-42) Afterwards, the young Mozart accompanied the Queen in an [aria](/wiki/Aria "Aria") that she sang, and played a solo work on the flute.[[39]](#cite_note-43) On 29 October, the Mozarts were in London again, and were invited to court to celebrate the fourth anniversary of the King's accession. As a memento of the royal favour, [Leopold Mozart](/wiki/Leopold_Mozart "Leopold Mozart") published six sonatas composed by Wolfgang, known as Mozart's [Opus 3](/wiki/Violin_Sonatas,_KV_10-15_(Mozart) "Violin Sonatas, KV 10-15 (Mozart)"), that were dedicated to the Queen on 18 January 1765, a dedication she rewarded with a present of 50 guineas.[[40]](#cite_note-44)

Charlotte was an amateur [botanist](/wiki/Botanist "Botanist") who took a great interest in [Kew Gardens](/wiki/Kew_Gardens "Kew Gardens"). In an age of discovery, when such travellers and explorers as [Captain James Cook](/wiki/Captain_James_Cook "Captain James Cook") and [Sir Joseph Banks](/wiki/Sir_Joseph_Banks "Sir Joseph Banks") were constantly bringing home new species and varieties of plants, she ensured that the collections were greatly enriched and expanded.[[41]](#cite_note-45) Her interest in botany led to the South African flower, the bird of paradise, being named *[Strelitzia reginae](/wiki/Strelitzia_reginae "Strelitzia reginae")* in her honour.[[42]](#cite_note-46)

Charlotte has also been credited with introducing the [Christmas tree](/wiki/Christmas_tree "Christmas tree") to Britain and its colonies.[[43]](#cite_note-charlotteobserver-47) Initially, Charlotte decorated a single [yew](/wiki/Yew "Yew") branch, a common Christmas tradition in her native Mecklenburg-Strelitz, to celebrate Christmas with members of the royal family and the royal household.[[44]](#cite_note-historytoday-48) She decorated the branch with the assistance of her ladies-in-waiting and then had the court gather to sing carols and distribute gifts.[[44]](#cite_note-historytoday-48) In December 1800, Queen Charlotte set up the first known English Christmas tree at [Queen's Lodge, Windsor](/wiki/Royal_Lodge "Royal Lodge").[[44]](#cite_note-historytoday-48)[[45]](#cite_note-independent-49) That year, she held a large Christmas party for the children of all the families in [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor,_Berkshire "Windsor, Berkshire") and placed a whole tree in the drawing-room, decorated with tinsel, glass, [baubles](/wiki/Baubles "Baubles") and fruits.[[44]](#cite_note-historytoday-48) [John Watkins](/wiki/John_Watkins_(writer) "John Watkins (writer)"), who attended the Christmas party, described the tree in his biography of the Queen: "from the branches of which hung bunches of sweetmeats, almonds and raisins in papers, fruits and toys, most tastefully arranged; the whole illuminated by small wax candles. After the company had walked round and admired the tree, each child obtained a portion of the sweets it bore, together with a toy, and then all returned home quite delighted."[[44]](#cite_note-historytoday-48) The practice of decorating a tree became popular among the British nobility and [gentry](/wiki/Landed_gentry "Landed gentry"), and later spread to the colonies.[[43]](#cite_note-charlotteobserver-47)[[44]](#cite_note-historytoday-48)

Among the royal couple's favoured craftsmen and artists were the cabinetmaker [William Vile](/wiki/William_Vile "William Vile"), silversmith [Thomas Heming](/wiki/Thomas_Heming "Thomas Heming"), the landscape designer [Capability Brown](/wiki/Capability_Brown "Capability Brown"), and the German painter [Johann Zoffany](/wiki/Johann_Zoffany "Johann Zoffany"), who frequently painted the King and Queen and their children in charmingly informal scenes, such as a portrait of Charlotte and her children as she sat at her dressing table.[[46]](#cite_note-50) In 1788, the royal couple visited the Worcester Porcelain Factory (founded in 1751, and later to be known as [Royal Worcester](/wiki/Royal_Worcester "Royal Worcester")), where Queen Charlotte ordered a porcelain service that was later renamed "Royal Lily" in her honour. Another well-known porcelain service designed and named in her honour was the "Queen Charlotte" pattern.[[47]](#cite_note-51)

The Queen founded orphanages and, in 1809, became the patron (providing new funding) of the General Lying-in Hospital, a hospital for expectant mothers. It was subsequently renamed as the Queen's Hospital, and is today the [Queen Charlotte's and Chelsea Hospital](/wiki/Queen_Charlotte%27s_and_Chelsea_Hospital "Queen Charlotte's and Chelsea Hospital").[[48]](#cite_note-Thomas1885-52)

Up until 1788, portraits of Charlotte often depict her in maternal poses with her children, and she looks young and contented;[[49]](#cite_note-53) however, that year, her husband fell seriously ill and became temporarily insane. It is now thought that the King had [porphyria](/wiki/Porphyria "Porphyria"),[[50]](#cite_note-54) though [bipolar disorder](/wiki/Bipolar_disorder "Bipolar disorder") has also been named as another possible underlying cause for his condition.[[51]](#cite_note-55)[[52]](#cite_note-56)[[53]](#cite_note-57) Sir [Thomas Lawrence](/wiki/Thomas_Lawrence "Thomas Lawrence")'s portrait of Charlotte at this time marks a transition point, after which she looks much older in her portraits; the assistant keeper of Charlotte's wardrobe, [Charlotte Papendiek](/wiki/Charlotte_Papendiek "Charlotte Papendiek"), wrote that the Queen was "much changed, her hair quite grey".[[54]](#cite_note-58)

## Friendship with Marie Antoinette

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charlotte_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Friendship with Marie Antoinette")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Queen_Charlotte_by_Sir_Thomas_Lawrence_1789.jpg/250px-Queen_Charlotte_by_Sir_Thomas_Lawrence_1789.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Charlotte_by_Sir_Thomas_Lawrence_1789.jpg)

Charlotte sat for Sir [Thomas Lawrence](/wiki/Thomas_Lawrence "Thomas Lawrence") in September 1789. His [portrait of her](/wiki/Portrait_of_Queen_Charlotte_(Lawrence) "Portrait of Queen Charlotte (Lawrence)") was exhibited at the Royal Academy the following year. Reviewers thought it "a strong likeness".[[55]](#cite_note-59)[[e]](#cite_note-60)

The [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") of 1789 probably added to the strain that Charlotte felt.[[56]](#cite_note-61) She had maintained a close relationship with Queen [Marie Antoinette](/wiki/Marie_Antoinette "Marie Antoinette") of France. Charlotte was 11 years older than Marie Antoinette, yet they shared many interests, such as their love of music and the arts, about which they were both enthusiastic. Never meeting face to face, they confined their friendship to pen and paper. Marie Antoinette confided in Charlotte upon the outbreak of the French Revolution. Charlotte had organized apartments to be prepared and ready for the refugee royal family of France to occupy.[[57]](#cite_note-62)
She was greatly distraught when she heard the news that the King and Queen of France had been executed.

## During the Regency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charlotte_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: During the Regency")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Stroehling_-_Queen_Charlotte%2C_1807%2C_Royal_Collection.jpg/250px-Stroehling_-_Queen_Charlotte%2C_1807%2C_Royal_Collection.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stroehling_-_Queen_Charlotte,_1807,_Royal_Collection.jpg)

Queen Charlotte in her later years, painted by [Stroehling](/wiki/Peter_Edward_Stroehling "Peter Edward Stroehling"), 1807, Royal Collection

After the onset of his permanent madness in 1811, George III was placed under the guardianship of his wife in accordance with the [Regency Bill of 1789](/wiki/Regency_Acts "Regency Acts").[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8)
She could not bring herself to visit him very often, due to his erratic behaviour and occasional violent reactions. It is believed she did not visit him again after June 1812. However, Charlotte remained supportive of her spouse as his illness worsened in old age. While her son, the [Prince Regent](/wiki/George_IV "George IV"), wielded the royal power, she was her spouse's legal guardian from 1811 until her death in 1818. Due to the extent of the King's illness, he was incapable of knowing or understanding that she had died.[[58]](#cite_note-63)

During the Regency of her son, Charlotte continued to fill her role as first lady in royal representation because of the estrangement of the Prince Regent and [his spouse](/wiki/Caroline_of_Brunswick "Caroline of Brunswick").[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8) As such, she functioned as the hostess by the side of her son at official receptions, such as the [festivities given in London](/wiki/Allied_sovereigns%27_visit_to_England "Allied sovereigns' visit to England") to celebrate the defeat of Emperor [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") in 1814.[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8) She also supervised the upbringing of her granddaughter, [Princess Charlotte of Wales](/wiki/Princess_Charlotte_of_Wales_(1796%E2%80%931817) "Princess Charlotte of Wales (1796–1817)").[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8)
During her last years, she was met with a growing lack of popularity and was sometimes subjected to demonstrations.[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8) After having attended a reception in London on 29 April 1817, she was jeered by a crowd. She told the crowd that it was upsetting to be treated like that after such long service.[[6]](#cite_note-Fitzgerald,_Percy_1899-8)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charlotte_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Death")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Inside_Kew_Palace_%28g%29_-_geograph.org.uk_-_4534916.jpg/250px-Inside_Kew_Palace_%28g%29_-_geograph.org.uk_-_4534916.jpg)](/wiki/File:Inside_Kew_Palace_(g)_-_geograph.org.uk_-_4534916.jpg)

Queen Charlotte's funerary hatchment on display at [Kew Palace](/wiki/Kew_Palace "Kew Palace"). The right is black and the left white because she was survived by her husband, King George III.

Charlotte died on 17 November 1818 at the age of 74 while seated in an armchair at Dutch House in Surrey, now known as [Kew Palace](/wiki/Kew_Palace "Kew Palace"). Her eldest son, the Prince Regent, was present, holding her hand.[[59]](#cite_note-64) She was buried in the Royal Vault at [St George's Chapel, Windsor Castle](/wiki/St_George%27s_Chapel,_Windsor_Castle "St George's Chapel, Windsor Castle") on 2 December.[[60]](#cite_note-65) Her husband died just over a year later. She is the longest-serving female consort and second-longest-serving consort in British history (after [Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh](/wiki/Prince_Philip,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh")), having served as such from her marriage (on 8 September 1761) to her death (17 November 1818), a total of 57 years and 70 days.[[61]](#cite_note-66)

On the day before her death, the Queen dictated her will to her husband's secretary, [Sir Herbert Taylor](/wiki/Sir_Herbert_Taylor "Sir Herbert Taylor"), appointing him and [Lord Arden](/wiki/Charles_Perceval,_2nd_Baron_Arden "Charles Perceval, 2nd Baron Arden") as her executors; at her death, her personal estate was valued at less than £140,000 (equivalent to £12,300,000 in 2023[[62]](#cite_note-inflation-UKGDP-67)), with her jewels accounting for the greater portion of her assets.[[63]](#cite_note-Queen_will-68) In her will, proven at [Doctor's Commons](/wiki/Doctor%27s_Commons "Doctor's Commons") on 8 January 1819, the Queen bequeathed her husband the jewels she had received from him, unless he remained in his state of insanity, in which case the jewels were to become an heirloom of the House of Hanover. Other jewels, including some given to Charlotte by [the Nawab of Arcot](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Khan_Wallajah "Muhammad Ali Khan Wallajah"), were to be evenly distributed among her surviving daughters. The furnishings and fixtures at the royal residence at Frogmore, along with "live and dead stock...on the estates", were bequeathed to her daughter [Augusta Sophia](/wiki/Augusta_Sophia "Augusta Sophia") along with the Frogmore property, unless its maintenance would prove too expensive for her daughter, in which case it was to revert to the Crown. Her daughter [Sophia](/wiki/Princess_Sophia_of_the_United_Kingdom "Princess Sophia of the United Kingdom") inherited the [Royal Lodge](/wiki/Royal_Lodge "Royal Lodge").[[63]](#cite_note-Queen_will-68) Certain personal assets that the Queen had brought from Mecklenburg-Strelitz were to revert to the senior branch of that dynasty, while the remainder of her assets, including her books, linen, art objects and china, were to be evenly divided among her surviving daughters.[[63]](#cite_note-Queen_will-68)

At the Queen's death, the Prince Regent claimed Charlotte's jewels, and on his death, they were in turn claimed by his heir, [William IV](/wiki/William_IV "William IV"). On William's death, Charlotte's bequest then sparked a protracted dispute between her granddaughter [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria"), who claimed the jewels as the property of the British Crown, and Charlotte's now eldest-surviving son [Ernest Augustus, King of Hanover](/wiki/Ernest_Augustus,_King_of_Hanover "Ernest Augustus, King of Hanover"), who claimed the jewels by right of being the most senior male member of the [House of Hanover](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover"). The dispute would not be resolved in Ernest's lifetime. Eventually in 1858, over twenty years after the death of William IV and nearly forty years after Charlotte's death, the matter was decided in favour of Ernest's son [George](/wiki/George_V_of_Hanover "George V of Hanover"), upon which Victoria had the jewels given into the custody of the Hanoverian ambassador.[[64]](#cite_note-69)

The rest of Charlotte's property was sold at auction from May to August 1819. Her clothes, furniture, and even her snuff were sold by [Christie's](/wiki/Christie%27s "Christie's").[[65]](#cite_note-70) It is highly unlikely that her husband ever knew of her death; he died blind, deaf, lame and insane 14 months later.[[66]](#cite_note-71)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charlotte_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Legacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Statue_probably_of_Queen_Charlotte%2C_Bloomsbury%2C_London.jpg/250px-Statue_probably_of_Queen_Charlotte%2C_Bloomsbury%2C_London.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statue_probably_of_Queen_Charlotte,_Bloomsbury,_London.jpg)

Statue in [Queen Square](/wiki/Queen_Square,_London "Queen Square, London"), London

Places named after Charlotte include the [Queen Charlotte Islands](/wiki/Queen_Charlotte_Islands "Queen Charlotte Islands") (now known as *Haida Gwaii*) in [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"), Canada, and [Queen Charlotte City](/wiki/Queen_Charlotte_City "Queen Charlotte City") (now known as *Daajing Giids*) on Haida Gwaii; [Queen Charlotte Sound](/wiki/Queen_Charlotte_Sound_(Canada) "Queen Charlotte Sound (Canada)") in British Columbia; [Queen Charlotte Channel](/wiki/Queen_Charlotte_Channel "Queen Charlotte Channel") (near [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), Canada); Queen Charlotte Bay in West Falkland; [Queen Charlotte Sound](/wiki/Queen_Charlotte_Sound_(New_Zealand) "Queen Charlotte Sound (New Zealand)"), South Island, New Zealand; [several fortifications](/wiki/Fort_Charlotte_(disambiguation) "Fort Charlotte (disambiguation)"), including [Fort Charlotte, Saint Vincent](/wiki/Fort_Charlotte,_Saint_Vincent "Fort Charlotte, Saint Vincent"); [Charlottesville, Virginia](/wiki/Charlottesville,_Virginia "Charlottesville, Virginia"); [Charlottetown, Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Charlottetown,_Prince_Edward_Island "Charlottetown, Prince Edward Island"); [Charlotte, North Carolina](/wiki/Charlotte,_North_Carolina "Charlotte, North Carolina");[[67]](#cite_note-Caucus-72) [Mecklenburg County, North Carolina](/wiki/Mecklenburg_County,_North_Carolina "Mecklenburg County, North Carolina"); [Mecklenburg County, Virginia](/wiki/Mecklenburg_County,_Virginia "Mecklenburg County, Virginia"); [Charlotte County, Virginia](/wiki/Charlotte_County,_Virginia "Charlotte County, Virginia"); [Charlotte County, Florida](/wiki/Charlotte_County,_Florida "Charlotte County, Florida"); [Port Charlotte, Florida](/wiki/Port_Charlotte,_Florida "Port Charlotte, Florida"); [Charlotte Harbor, Florida](/wiki/Charlotte_Harbor,_Florida "Charlotte Harbor, Florida"); and [Charlotte, Vermont](/wiki/Charlotte,_Vermont "Charlotte, Vermont"). The proposed [North American colonies](/wiki/British_America "British America") of [Vandalia](/wiki/Vandalia_(colony) "Vandalia (colony)")[[68]](#cite_note-73)[[69]](#cite_note-74)[[70]](#cite_note-75) and [Charlotina](/wiki/Charlotina "Charlotina") were also named for her.[[71]](#cite_note-76) In Tonga, the royal family adopted the name Sālote (the Tongan version of Charlotte) in her honour, and notable individuals included [Sālote Lupepauʻu](/wiki/S%C4%81lote_Lupepau%CA%BBu "Sālote Lupepauʻu") and [Sālote Tupou III](/wiki/S%C4%81lote_Tupou_III "Sālote Tupou III").[[72]](#cite_note-77)

Charlotte's provision of funding to the General Lying-in Hospital in London prevented its closure; today it is named [Queen Charlotte's and Chelsea Hospital](/wiki/Queen_Charlotte%27s_and_Chelsea_Hospital "Queen Charlotte's and Chelsea Hospital"), and is an acknowledged centre of excellence amongst maternity hospitals. A large copy of the [Allan Ramsay](/wiki/Allan_Ramsay_(artist) "Allan Ramsay (artist)") portrait of Queen Charlotte hangs in the main lobby of the hospital.[[48]](#cite_note-Thomas1885-52) The [Queen Charlotte's Ball](/wiki/Queen_Charlotte%27s_Ball "Queen Charlotte's Ball"), an annual debutante ball that originally funded the hospital, is named after her.[[73]](#cite_note-78)

A lead statue probably of Charlotte, dating to c. 1775, stands on [Queen Square](/wiki/Queen_Square,_London "Queen Square, London") in [Bloomsbury](/wiki/Bloomsbury "Bloomsbury"), London,[[74]](#cite_note-79)[[75]](#cite_note-80) and there are two statues of her in [Charlotte](/wiki/Charlotte,_North_Carolina "Charlotte, North Carolina"), North Carolina: at [Charlotte Douglas International Airport](/wiki/Charlotte_Douglas_International_Airport "Charlotte Douglas International Airport")[[76]](#cite_note-81) and at the International Trade Center.[[77]](#cite_note-82)

[Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey](/wiki/Rutgers,_The_State_University_of_New_Jersey "Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey"), was chartered in 1766 as *Queen's College*, in reference to Queen Charlotte.[[78]](#cite_note-83) It was renamed in 1825 in honour of [Henry Rutgers](/wiki/Henry_Rutgers "Henry Rutgers"), a Revolutionary War officer and college benefactor. Its oldest extant building, [Old Queen's](/wiki/Old_Queen%27s "Old Queen's") (built 1809–1823), and the city block that forms the historic core of the university, [Queen's Campus](/wiki/Queens_Campus,_Rutgers_University "Queens Campus, Rutgers University"), retain their original names.[[79]](#cite_note-QueensCampus1973NRHPApp-84)

Queen Charlotte was played by [Frances White](/wiki/Frances_White_(actress) "Frances White (actress)") in the 1979 television series *[Prince Regent](/wiki/Prince_Regent_(TV_series) "Prince Regent (TV series)")*, by [Helen Mirren](/wiki/Helen_Mirren "Helen Mirren") in the 1994 film *[The Madness of King George](/wiki/The_Madness_of_King_George "The Madness of King George")*,[[80]](#cite_note-85) by [Golda Rosheuvel](/wiki/Golda_Rosheuvel "Golda Rosheuvel") in the 2020 [Netflix original](/wiki/Netflix_original "Netflix original") series *[Bridgerton](/wiki/Bridgerton "Bridgerton")*,[[81]](#cite_note-86) and by [India Amarteifio](/wiki/India_Amarteifio "India Amarteifio") in her younger years and Rosheuvel, in her older years, in *[Queen Charlotte: A Bridgerton Story](/wiki/Queen_Charlotte:_A_Bridgerton_Story "Queen Charlotte: A Bridgerton Story")*.[[82]](#cite_note-87)[[83]](#cite_note-88)[[84]](#cite_note-89)

*[Strelitzia](/wiki/Strelitzia "Strelitzia")*, a genus of flowering plants native to South Africa that has become ubiquitous in warm-weather regions worldwide, is named for Charlotte's native Mecklenburg-Strelitz.[[85]](#cite_note-90)

## Arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charlotte_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Arms")]

The [royal coat of arms of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Royal_coat_of_arms_of_the_United_Kingdom "Royal coat of arms of the United Kingdom") are impaled with her father's arms as a [Duke of Mecklenburg-Strelitz](/wiki/Duke_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz "Duke of Mecklenburg-Strelitz"). The arms were: Quarterly of six, 1st, Or, a buffalo's head cabossed Sable, armed and ringed Argent, crowned and langued Gules ([Mecklenburg](/wiki/Mecklenburg "Mecklenburg")); 2nd, Azure, a griffin segreant Or ([Rostock](/wiki/Rostock "Rostock")); 3rd, Per fess, in chief Azure, a griffin segreant Or, and in the base Vert, a bordure Argent ([Principality of Schwerin](/wiki/Duchy_of_Mecklenburg-Schwerin "Duchy of Mecklenburg-Schwerin")); 4th, Gules, a cross patée Argent crowned Or ([Ratzeburg](/wiki/Ratzeburg "Ratzeburg")); 5th, Gules, a dexter arm Argent issuant from clouds in sinister flank and holding a finger ring Or ([County of Schwerin](/wiki/County_of_Schwerin "County of Schwerin")); 6th, Or, a buffalo's head Sable, armed Argent, crowned and langued Gules ([Wenden](/wiki/Werle "Werle")); Overall an inescutcheon, per fess Gules and Or ([Stargard](/wiki/Burg_Stargard "Burg Stargard")).[[86]](#cite_note-91)

The Queen's arms changed twice to mirror the changes in her husband's arms, once in 1801 and then again in 1816. A [funerary hatchment](/wiki/Funerary_hatchment "Funerary hatchment") displaying the Queen's full [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms"), painted in 1818, is on display at [Kew Palace](/wiki/Kew_Palace "Kew Palace").[[87]](#cite_note-92)[[88]](#cite_note-93)

## African ancestry claim

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charlotte_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: African ancestry claim")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Margarita_de_Castro_e_Souza_genealogy_and_descent.JPG/250px-Margarita_de_Castro_e_Souza_genealogy_and_descent.JPG)](/wiki/File:Margarita_de_Castro_e_Souza_genealogy_and_descent.JPG)

One of three lines of descent between Charlotte, Margarita de Castro e Sousa, and [Madragana](/wiki/Madragana "Madragana"). This portion of Charlotte's ancestry has often been used to justify claims that she was of African descent.[[90]](#cite_note-Motlalepula-95)

Claims that Queen Charlotte may have had partial [African](/wiki/Demographics_of_Africa "Demographics of Africa") ancestry first emerged in *Racial Mixture as the Basic Principle of Life* published in 1929 by German historian, Brunold Springer, who challenged her Thomas Gainsborough portrait as inaccurate.[[91]](#cite_note-gregory-96)

Based on her alternative portrait by [Allan Ramsay](/wiki/Allan_Ramsay_(artist) "Allan Ramsay (artist)") and contemporary descriptions of her appearance, Springer concluded that Charlotte's "broad nostrils and heavy lips" must point to African heritage. Jamaican-American amateur historian [J. A. Rogers](/wiki/Joel_Augustus_Rogers "Joel Augustus Rogers") agreed with Springer in his 1940 book *Sex and Race: Volume I*,[[92]](#cite_note-sex_and_race-97)[[93]](#cite_note-indianexpress.com-98) where he concluded that Queen Charlotte must be "biracial"[[94]](#cite_note-blakemore-99) or "black".[[90]](#cite_note-Motlalepula-95)[[95]](#cite_note-jefferies-100)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Allan_Ramsay_%281713-84%29_-_Queen_Charlotte_%281744-1818%29_-_RCIN_405308_-_Royal_Collection.jpg/250px-Allan_Ramsay_%281713-84%29_-_Queen_Charlotte_%281744-1818%29_-_RCIN_405308_-_Royal_Collection.jpg)](/wiki/File:Allan_Ramsay_(1713-84)_-_Queen_Charlotte_(1744-1818)_-_RCIN_405308_-_Royal_Collection.jpg)

Queen Charlotte by [Allan Ramsay](/wiki/Allan_Ramsay_(artist) "Allan Ramsay (artist)"), 1762

Proponents of the African ancestry claim also hold to a literal interpretation of [Baron Stockmar](/wiki/Baron_Stockmar "Baron Stockmar")'s diary, in which he described Charlotte as "small and crooked, with a real [Mulatto](/wiki/Mulatto "Mulatto") face". Stockmar, who served as personal physician to the Queen's grandson-in-law, [Leopold I of Belgium](/wiki/Leopold_I_of_Belgium "Leopold I of Belgium"), arrived at court just two years before Charlotte's death in 1816. His descriptions of Charlotte's children in this same diary are equally unflattering.[[96]](#cite_note-Christian_Friedrich_von_Stockmar-101)

In 1997, Mario de Valdes y Cocom, a genealogist and self-described "independent researcher",[[97]](#cite_note-102)[[98]](#cite_note-Ungoed-Thomas-103) popularized and expanded on earlier arguments in an article for [PBS *Frontline*](/wiki/Frontline_(American_TV_program) "Frontline (American TV program)"),[[99]](#cite_note-valdes-104) which has since been cited as the main source by a number of articles on the topic.[[100]](#cite_note-brown-105)[[101]](#cite_note-106)[[102]](#cite_note-107)[[103]](#cite_note-108) Valdes also seized on Charlotte's 1762 Allan Ramsay portrait as evidence of African ancestry, citing the Queen's "unmistakable African appearance" and "[negroid](/wiki/Negroid "Negroid") physiogomy" [*[sic](/wiki/Sic "Sic")*].[[99]](#cite_note-valdes-104)

Valdes claimed that Charlotte had inherited these features from one of her distant ancestors, [Madragana](/wiki/Madragana "Madragana") (born c. 1230), a mistress of King [Afonso III of Portugal](/wiki/Afonso_III_of_Portugal "Afonso III of Portugal") (c. 1210 – 1279).[[104]](#cite_note-pbs-109) His conclusion is based on various historical sources that describe Madragana as either [Moorish](/wiki/Moorish "Moorish")[[105]](#cite_note-110) or [Mozarab](/wiki/Mozarab "Mozarab"),[[106]](#cite_note-111) which Valdes erroneously interpreted to mean that she was black.[[95]](#cite_note-jefferies-100)

Although popular among the general public, the claims are rejected by most scholars.[[107]](#cite_note-:0-112)[[93]](#cite_note-indianexpress.com-98)[[108]](#cite_note-Lisa_Hilton-113)[[109]](#cite_note-Jill_Sudbury-114)[[95]](#cite_note-jefferies-100) Aside from Stockmar's jab at her appearance shortly before her death, Charlotte was never referred to as having any specifically African physical features, let alone ancestry, during her lifetime. Furthermore, her portraiture was not atypical for her time, and painted portraits in general should not be considered reliable evidence of a sitter's true appearance.[[109]](#cite_note-Jill_Sudbury-114) Historian [Andrew Roberts](/wiki/Andrew_Roberts_(historian) "Andrew Roberts (historian)") describes the claims as "utter rubbish", and attributes its public popularity to a hesitancy among historians to openly address it due to its "[cultural cringe](/wiki/Cultural_cringe "Cultural cringe") factor".[[107]](#cite_note-:0-112)

The use of the term "Moor" as a racial identifier for Charlotte's ancestor Madragana is also inconclusive as during the Middle Ages the term was not used to describe race but religious affiliation.[[110]](#cite_note-115)[[111]](#cite_note-116) Regardless, Madragana was more likely an Iberian Mozarab,[[112]](#cite_note-117)[[113]](#cite_note-118)[[114]](#cite_note-119)[[115]](#cite_note-120) and any genetic contribution from an ancestor fifteen generations removed would be so diluted as to have a negligible effect on her appearance.[[108]](#cite_note-Lisa_Hilton-113)[[95]](#cite_note-jefferies-100)
